name: Extremaduran

ancestors.notes: Standard Castilian Spanish: primary ancestor and prestige norm. Extremaduran developed in the 13th–15th c. as Castilian spread southward into Extremadura, mixing with pre-existing Leonese/Asturleonese settlers from the Reconquista repopulation.

Asturleonese/Leonese adstrate: Extremadura was originally repopulated largely by settlers from León and the Leonese meseta in the 12th–14th c. Leonese features persist as substrate: F- aspiration (fazer→hacer→[ˈhaθeɾ]), strong aspiration of word-final /s/, some archaic diphthongisation in conservative rural registers.

Galaico-Portuguese western border contact: Extremadura shares a long border with Portugal (Alentejo, Beira Baixa). The western zone (Valencia de Alcántara, Olivenza border area) shows Portuguese-contact features including seseo and vocalic influence. Olivençan Portuguese itself is a distinct contact variety.

Andalusi Arabic: pre-Reconquista population of Extremadura was largely Arabophone Mozarabic or Muslim; Arabic-origin place names are abundant (Mérida, Cáceres, Badajoz, Alburquerque, Almendralejo). Limited phonological influence.
Mozarabic substrate: Extremaduran Mozarabs were absorbed into the Castilian/Leonese repopulation. Some conservative Ibero-Romance features may reflect Mozarabic conservatism.
Vettonian/Celtiberian substrate: pre-Roman Celtic-speaking population of Extremadura (Vettones). Primarily lexical (some place names).
Lusitanian substrate: pre-Roman Celtic-speaking population of Extremadura. Primarily lexical (some place names).

notes: Extremaduran (extremeñu) — the transitional Castilian-Leonese dialect of the Autonomous Community of Extremadura (Badajoz and Cáceres provinces). Classified variously as a southern dialect of Castilian or as an independent Ibero-Romance variety. KEY FEATURES: (1) SESEO (complete in most areas): /θ/ = /s/ — ⟨c⟩ before e/i and ⟨z⟩ → /s/, merging distinción into seseo. Some northern Cáceres retains partial distinción under Castilian prestige pressure. (2) ASPIRATION OF WORD-FINAL AND PRE-CONSONANTAL /s/: coda /s/ → [h] or deleted — one of the most salient features; 'estos hombres' [ˈehtoh ˈõmbɾeh]. (3) F- ASPIRATION (rural/archaic): Latin F- → [h] in many rural areas (hacer [ˈhaθeɾ], hijo [ˈhiho]) — shared with Andalusian and attributed to Leonese substrate (Basque-contact F→H change). (4) YEÍSMO: ⟨ll⟩ = ⟨y⟩ = /j/ or /ʝ/ (majority of speakers; some older rural registers retain /ʎ/). (5) WORD-FINAL /d/ → [∅]: verdad [berˈdá] (complete elision more frequent than standard Castilian [ð]). (6) STRONG INTERVOCALIC LENITION: continued from Castilian, with further weakening in rapid speech. (7) /x/ → [h]: the velar fricative weakens to [h] in many Extremaduran registers — 'jota' [ˈhota]. Note: Extremaduran (estremeñu) is also used to refer to a distinct Asturleonese-descendant dialect spoken in the Sierra de Gata/Campo Arañuelo area (closer to ast-PT-x-medieval); this JSON models the Castilian-base variety. Sources: González Salgado (2000) *Cartografía lingüística de Extremadura*, Martínez González (2002), Zamora Vicente (1960) *Dialectología española*.

sources.author: Penny, R.
